input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab-scale gantry crane physical system consisting of a mechanical crane structure and a controller, operating in a research and educational domain. Key characteristics include public availability, reliance on open-source software, and suitability for cyber-physical system research due to its simplicity, reproducibility, and applicability to continuous validation, fault detection, and system evolution studies.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include optimal control for trajectory planning, historical data logging for system state tracking, data visualization for operational monitoring, and continuous validation to ensure synchronization between physical and digital components. These services target researchers and educators, enabling validation of digital twin methodologies and educational demonstrations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Python-based gantrylib module for C4 Container integration, Docker containerization of TimescaleDB (database), Eclipse Mosquitto (MQTT broker), and Grafana (dashboard). Physical controller implemented using Arduino hardware, with all tools being open-source and commonly used to ensure reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture of the lab scale gantry crane system is described using the C4 Model [5] with context and container diagrams to visualize software architecture components and their interactions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin's computational models consist of CAD models and a kinematic model of the gantry crane, with simulation services running on a TimescaleDB database for time-series data management. The architecture is visualized using the C4 Model for software architecture description.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Two motors with associated controllers for movement along gantry axes and an electromagnet mounted on the hoist for lifting and transporting container-like pucks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: A Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist and an Anemometer measures wind speed for environmental monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service compares real-time sensor measurements with simulated trajectories and checks deviations against predefined thresholds to ensure model validity and system safety.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the physical crane is collected by the Physical Crane Interface (Python program) and published to an MQTT broker. The Logging Application subscribes to MQTT topics to continuously log crane state data into a TimescaleDB database. Specific data types, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and integration mechanisms beyond MQTT and TimescaleDB are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Trajectory Generator Service utilizes a kinematic model to generate optimal movement trajectories, which are communicated to the Control Application via MQTT. The Control Application then uses the Physical Crane Interface to execute these commands on the physical crane. Command formats, validation mechanisms, conflict resolution, and execution confirmation details are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Digital Twin services communicate using MQTT protocol through an MQTT broker for signaling. Network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin system is deployed on a personal computer (on-premise), with the Database, MQTT Broker, and Dashboard components containerized using Docker. Computational and storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, and detailed availability/reliability assurances are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Air Quality Management exemplar employs Model-Driven digital twin engineering methodologies, while GreenhouseDT and the lab scale gantry crane use an ad-hoc approach without commercial digital twin frameworks. Version control is managed via GitHub repositories, and containerization/virtualization technologies (e.g., virtual machines) are utilized for deployment and dissemination.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar provides runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction capabilities for indoor air quality management in virus spread prevention contexts. Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the digital twin pipeline, but specific analytics processing details, user interaction methods, and confidence measures for recommendations are not described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: AutomationML is used for the digital twin model in the Air Quality Management exemplar. The C4 Model is applied for architecture visualization in the lab scale gantry crane system. No other industry standards or compliance frameworks are specified in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the description of the physical twin, including its components, operational domain, and suitability characteristics. It is strongly supported by the abstract and introduction which describe the system as a lab-scale gantry crane for research and education, noting its public availability and use in specific studies like fault detection and system evolution. Quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin... The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller', 'publicly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software', 'used as use case... continuous validation methods, fault detection methods... DarTwin, a notation for system evolution'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific services offered by the digital twin, aligning them with their functions (e.g., optimal control for trajectory planning) and identifying the target users. The abstract explicitly lists these services, and Section III provides technical details on the trajectory generator, logging, dashboard, and continuous validation. Quotes: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Trajectory Generator Service... generates optimal movement trajectories', 'Continuous Validation Service... continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories'.

The extraction accurately identifies the specific software tools (gantrylib, Docker, TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto, Grafana) and hardware (Arduino) used in the implementation. It correctly highlights the open-source nature of these tools. Supported by quotes: 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib', 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker', and 'Arduino measures and controls those last three components'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions general data flow and 'real-time status' but does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, time scales, latency requirements, or temporal granularity as defined by the characteristic. Therefore, the assessment that this information is not present in the document is correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes a single physical gantry crane and a single digital twin instance. While teaching extensions are mentioned, the core system architecture does not describe multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins. Thus, the extraction accurately reflects the absence of this information.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of the C4 Model for architecture visualization and mentions context and container diagrams. However, the description lacks depth regarding component integration, resource management (e.g., specific details on Docker containerization or the role of the MQTT Broker), and coordination mechanisms (e.g., how services interact via the broker). It is specific but could be expanded with more technical details present in the source.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The Source Documents focus on the internal architecture of the gantry crane system and its digital twin (e.g., Python applications, MQTT Broker). There is no discussion of integration with external systems, enterprise systems, or interoperability standards. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' claim is correct.

logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the specific model types (CAD and kinematic) and data management infrastructure (TimescaleDB) mentioned in the text, as well as the architecture visualization method (C4 Model). Supported by quotes: 'The digital side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database... optimized for time series data', and 'we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture of the system'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific actuators (two motors), their controllers, the end-effector (electromagnet), and the object manipulated (pucks/containers). Supported by quotes: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement', 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers', and 'It moves small pucks which represents containers'.

The description precisely lists the sensors and their specific measurement functions as described in the source text. Supported by quotes: 'a Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist', and 'an Anemometer measures the wind speed'.

The characteristic accurately describes the Continuous Validation Service, including its method of comparing real-time measurements against simulations and its threshold-checking mechanism. Supported by quotes: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories', and 'It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the components and data flow described in Section III.A. It correctly notes the role of the Physical Crane Interface ('a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane'), the Logging Application ('continuously logs the crane’s state to the database'), and the use of TimescaleDB. It appropriately identifies MQTT as the protocol (implied by 'MQTT Broker for signaling') and honestly lists the missing technical details (frequencies, triggers, etc.) which are indeed absent from the text.

The description accurately reflects the control architecture presented in the text. It correctly cites the Trajectory Generator Service which 'utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories' and the Control Application which 'uses the crane interface to control the crane.' The extraction accurately notes that specific command formats and validation details are not provided in the source text.

The extraction correctly identifies that 'Digital Twin services communicate using MQTT protocol through an MQTT broker for signaling' based on the text 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.' It also correctly states that deeper network details like 'Network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols' are not detailed in the document, as the text focuses on a local setup.

The extraction accurately describes the deployment on a 'personal computer' and the use of 'Docker' for containerization of specific components (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard). This is directly supported by the source: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer' and 'the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker'.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
